message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Using provisional or opportunistic data, three nationwide studies (The Netherlands, USA, and Denmark) have identified a reduction in preterm or extremely preterm births during periods of COVID-19 restrictions. However, these findings have been challenged as none of the studies accounted for perinatal deaths.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine whether the reduction in extremely preterm births, observed in Denmark during the COVID-19 lockdown, could be a result of an increase in number of perinatal deaths; and to assess the impact of extended COVID-19 restrictions on the prevalence of preterm birth and perinatal death.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The study is a nationwide Danish register-based prevalence proportion study using detailed data to capture all births (induced abortions were excluded) throughout Denmark. We assessed the prevalence of stillbirth, preterm birth, and early neonatal death.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Population-based study</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>All singleton pregnancies delivered in Denmark, between February 27, and September 30, 2015-2020. COVID-19 lockdown was studied in 31,164 births and the extended period of COVID-19 restrictions in 214,862 births.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 restrictions broadly (February 27, – September 30, 2020) or COVID-19 lockdown specifically (March 12, – April 14, 2020).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Prevalence of preterm births, stillbirths and early neonatal deaths across the periods under study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The extremely preterm birth rate was reduced (OR 0.27, 95% CI 0.07 to 0.86) during the strict lockdown period, while perinatal mortality was not significantly different. During the extended period of restrictions, the extremely preterm birth rate was marginally reduced, and a significant reduction in the stillbirth rate (OR 0.69, 0.50 to 0.95) was observed. No changes in early neonatal mortality rates were found.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Stillbirth and extremely preterm birth rates were reduced in Denmark during the period of COVID-19 restrictions and lockdown, respectively, suggesting that aspects of these containment and control measures confer an element of protection. The present observational study does not allow for causal inference; however, the results support design of studies to ascertain whether behavioural or social changes for pregnant women may improve pregnancy outcomes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>None</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Can changes in stillbirth and early neonatal mortality rates during the COVID-19 lockdown explain the reduction in extremely preterm birth seen in Denmark?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>In this nationwide register-based study that included data pertaining to 245,999 pregnancies, the statistically significant reduction in extremely preterm birth during the COVID-19 lockdown was confirmed. The stillbirth and early neonatal mortality rates were unchanged during lockdown, while the stillbirth rate was reduced over the extended period of COVID-19 restrictions.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>The reduction in extremely preterm births seen during the COVID-19 lockdown in Denmark is not a result of increased stillbirth or early neonatal death.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Preterm birth, stillbirth, and early neonatal mortality during the Danish COVID-19 lockdown
